TITLE: API Documentation Inaccurately Displays Keyword Argument Signatures BODY: I've encountered a problem where the API documentation for methods is missing the `**` indicator for keyword arguments. This omission makes it very difficult to correctly understand and use the API, as the documented signatures appear to show direct parameters when they are actually part of a `**kwargs` collection. This led to significant confusion when I was trying to call `model_construct(values={...})`. The call wasn't working, and I spent a considerable amount of time debugging before realizing that the documentation had simply removed the `**` from the method's signature. The actual source code clearly shows `values` being passed via `**kwargs`, but the docs imply it's a named parameter. The discrepancy between the true method signatures in the source code and their representation in the documentation causes developers to make incorrect assumptions and waste time troubleshooting. --- **Actual method signature** https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/5d4a3c8cb356fcdad925b7276b5338c24e6cf653/pydantic/main.py#L317
**Method signature in documentation, missing `**`**

Cannot use `None` as type argument

### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

Pydantic's BaseModel.__class_getitem__ is annotated as `typevar_values: type[Any] | tuple[type[Any], ...]`, which causes type checkers to reject `None` as a type argument even though `None` is a valid PEP 484 type
annotation meaning `type(None)` / `NoneType`.

Error in PyCharm: `Expected type 'type | tuple[type, ...]', got 'type[None]'`

Root cause: pydantic/main.py BaseModel.__class_getitem__ signature should accept
`None` (and other special forms) as valid type arguments, not just `type[Any]`.

We could use `NoneType` to get rid of the problem, however, I suggest we update annotation on pydantic side.

### Example Code

```Python
import pydantic as p


class Box[T](p.BaseModel):
    pass


# Triggers the warning because Pydantic's __class_getitem__ is typed as
# typevar_values: type[Any] | tuple[type[Any], ...]
# and None is not type[Any]:
x = Box[None]()
```

pydocstyle domain hijacked — replace with ruff in contributing guide


  ## Problem

  The contributing guide references pydocstyle (https://www.pydocstyle.org) for docstring linting.
  The pydocstyle project is no longer actively maintained and its domain has been hijacked.

  ## Solution

  Replace all pydocstyle references in `docs/contributing.md` with ruff, which Pydantic already
  uses for docstring linting. Update instructions to reflect actual tooling.

  ## Changes needed

  - Replace pydocstyle linting mention with ruff in `docs/contributing.md`
  - Update conflict resolution hint to reference ruff instead of pydocstyle

Support `mariadbconnector_async` dialect in `MariaDBDsn`

### Initial Checks

- [x] I have searched Google & GitHub for similar requests and couldn't find anything
- [x] I have read and followed [the docs](https://pydantic.dev/docs/validation/latest/get-started/) and still think this feature is missing

## Requested feature

The official MariaDB Connector introduced native async support using the async dialect. `MariaDBDsn` should accept `mariadb+mariadbconnector_async` as a valid scheme alongside the existing dialects.

I believe `AnyUrl` doesn't allow underscores, which might complicate the implementation a bit

```python
>>> from pydantic import TypeAdapter
>>> from pydantic.networks import MariaDBDsn
>>> validate_mariadb = TypeAdapter(MariaDBDsn)
>>> validate_mariadb.validate_python("mariadb+mariadbconnector://user:pass@localhost:3306/db")
MariaDBDsn('mariadb+mariadbconnector://user:pass@localhost:3306/db')
>>> validate_mariadb.validate_python("mariadb+mariadbconnector_async://user:pass@localhost:3306/db")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/jape/tech/koodit/testing/.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/pydantic/type_adapter.py", line 441, in validate_python
    return self.validator.validate_python(
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for function-wrap[wrap_val()]
  Input should be a valid URL, relative URL without a base [type=url_parsing, input_value='mariadb+mariadbconnector...:pass@localhost:3306/db', input_type=str]
    For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.13/v/url_parsing
```


## Current MariaDBDsn

https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/5d4a3c8cb356fcdad925b7276b5338c24e6cf653/pydantic/networks.py#L938-L949

## Mariadb async dialect

https://mariadb.com/resources/blog/mariadb-python-connector-2-0-0rc2/

```python
class MySQLDialect_mariadbconnector_async(MySQLDialect_mariadbconnector):
    """
    Async dialect for MariaDB Connector/Python.

    Uses lightweight wrapper objects that adapt the async DBAPI to
    SQLAlchemy's sync-style interface via await_only(). Works with
    both mariadb (pure Python) and mariadb_c (C extension) backends.

    Usage:
        from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine

        engine = create_async_engine(
            "mariadb+mariadbconnector_async://user:pass@host/db"
        )
    """

    driver = "mariadbconnector_async"
```

https://github.com/mariadb-corporation/mariadb-connector-python/blob/0243821280acfc6a238066025fbaf094253b7b17/mariadb/sqlalchemy_mariadb_async.py#L271

TITLE: TypeAdapter fails to apply schema for union type serialization

BODY:
When attempting to serialize data to JSON using Pydantic's `TypeAdapter` with a union type, the serialization process does not behave as expected. Instead of adhering to the provided schema for the union, the system appears to serialize the underlying object directly. This results in incorrect JSON output and triggers `UserWarning` messages, specifically `PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue`, indicating that the serializer received an input value of a type different from what it anticipated based on the schema.

For example, the warnings observed are:
```
PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `TestSchema` - serialized value may not be as expected [input_value=TestModel(id=1, name='Obj...', derived_from_id=None), input_type=TestModel])
PydanticSerializationUnexpectedValue(Expected `DifferentTestSchema` - serialized value may not be as expected [input_value=[TestModel(id=1, name='Ob...ct', derived_from_id=1)], input_type=list])
```
This behavior has been observed while using Pydantic V2.

StrEnum instances no longer normalised to str instances when used as dict keys in dynamic model creation

### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

In 2.12.5, if using a `StrEnum` instance as the key of a dict being passed to `create_model`, that instance would be normalised to a `str` instance before being set as the field name on the dynamically created model. In 2.13.4, it remains as a `StrEnum` instance. 

For example: 
- Create a `main.py`:
```python
import pydantic, enum

class MyStrEnum(enum.StrEnum):
    sender = 'sender'

inner = pydantic.create_model('Inner', x=(str, pydantic.Field(default=None)))
Model = pydantic.create_model('M', **{MyStrEnum.sender: (inner, pydantic.Field(default=None))})

try:
    Model.model_validate({'sender': {'x': 123}})
except pydantic.ValidationError as e:
    loc0 = e.errors()[0]['loc'][0]
    print(f'loc[0]={loc0!r}  type={type(loc0).__name__}  is str={type(loc0) is str}')
```
- Install pydantic 2.12.5 and run the file
```bash
uv add pydantic==2.12.5
uv run main.py
```
- Observe the following output
```
loc[0]='sender'  type=str  is str=True
```
- Now install 2.13.4 and run the file
```bash
uv add pydantic==2.13.4
uv run main.py
```
- Observe the following output
```
loc[0]=<MyStrEnum.sender: 'sender'>  type=MyStrEnum  is str=False
```

Suspected miscompilation with `bool as u32`

Godbolt link: https://godbolt.org/z/G4hxdn4Wa

I tried this code (simplified to a ~minimal version):

```rust
struct Store {
    ring: [Kind; 32],
}

#[derive(Copy, Clone, Eq, PartialEq)]
enum Kind {
    A,
    B,
    C
}

struct Ring(u32);

impl Ring {
    fn peek(&self, store: &Store) -> Kind {
        store.ring[(self.0 as usize) % 32]
    }

    pub fn peek_test(&self, store: &Store, expected: Kind) -> bool {
        self.peek(store) == expected
    }

    #[inline]
    pub fn consume_test(&mut self, store: &Store, expected: Kind) -> bool {
        let x = self.peek_test(store, expected);
        self.0 += if x { 1 } else { 0 };
        x
    }

    // Uses a u32 cast instead of the 
    #[inline]
    pub fn consume_test_borked(&mut self, store: &Store, expected: Kind) -> bool {
        let x = self.peek_test(store, expected);
        self.0 += x as u32;
        x
    }
}

#[unsafe(no_mangle)]
fn foo(store: &Store, mut lex: Ring) -> usize {
    if !lex.consume_test(store, Kind::A) {
        return 0;
    }
    match lex.peek(store) {
        Kind::A => 17,
        _ => 27,
    }
}

#[unsafe(no_mangle)]
fn foo_borked(store: &Store, mut lex: Ring) -> usize {
    if !lex.consume_test_borked(store, Kind::A) {
        return 0;
    }
    match lex.peek(store) {
        Kind::A => 17,
        _ => 27,
    }
}
```

I expected `foo` and `foo_borked` to have identical behavior/codegen.

Instead, this happened: `foo_borked` compiled into a function that always takes the `Kind::A` branch, never returning 27.

```asm
; rustc -Copt-level=3 [also =2]

foo:
        mov     eax, esi
        and     eax, 31
        cmp     byte ptr [rdi + rax], 0
        je      .LBB0_2
        xor     eax, eax
        ret
.LBB0_2:
        inc     esi
        and     esi, 31
        cmp     byte ptr [rdi + rsi], 0
        mov     ecx, 17
        mov     eax, 27
        cmove   rax, rcx
        ret

foo_borked:
        and     esi, 31
        xor     ecx, ecx
        cmp     byte ptr [rdi + rsi], 0
        mov     eax, 17
        cmovne  rax, rcx
        ret
```

Looking at the LLVM IR with `-Cno-prepopulate-passes` it appears that this is a rustc bug, and given that `-Zmir-opt-level=0` produces valid code (`foo_borked = foo`) this is likely a Mir opt issue.

```llvm
define noundef i64 @foo_borked(ptr noalias nofree noundef readonly captures(address, read_provenance) dereferenceable(32) %store, i32 noundef %0) unnamed_addr {
start:
  %__self_discr.dbg.spill = alloca [8 x i8], align 8
  %__arg1_discr.dbg.spill = alloca [8 x i8], align 8
  %expected.dbg.spill1 = alloca [1 x i8], align 1
  %expected.dbg.spill = alloca [1 x i8], align 1
  %store.dbg.spill = alloca [8 x i8], align 8
  %x = alloca [1 x i8], align 1
  %_0 = alloca [8 x i8], align 8
  %lex = alloca [4 x i8], align 4
  store i32 %0, ptr %lex, align 4
  store ptr %store, ptr %store.dbg.spill, align 8
  store i8 0, ptr %expected.dbg.spill, align 1
  store i8 0, ptr %expected.dbg.spill1, align 1
  store i64 0, ptr %__arg1_discr.dbg.spill, align 8
  call void @llvm.lifetime.start.p0(ptr %x)
  %_14 = load i32, ptr %lex, align 4
  %_13 = zext i32 %_14 to i64
  %_12 = urem i64 %_13, 32
  %_15 = icmp ult i64 %_12, 32
  br i1 %_15, label %bb7, label %panic

bb7:
  %1 = getelementptr inbounds nuw i8, ptr %store, i64 %_12
  %_10 = load i8, ptr %1, align 1
  %__self_discr = zext i8 %_10 to i64
  store i64 %__self_discr, ptr %__self_discr.dbg.spill, align 8
  ; %x is being loaded here without a prior store, becoming a poison
  %2 = load i8, ptr %x, align 1
  %3 = trunc nuw i8 %2 to i1
  %4 = icmp ule i1 %3, true
  call void @llvm.assume(i1 %4)
  %_8 = zext i1 %3 to i32
  %5 = load i32, ptr %lex, align 4
  %6 = add i32 %5, %_8
  store i32 %6, ptr %lex, align 4
  %7 = icmp eq i64 %__self_discr, 0
  br i1 %7, label %bb1, label %bb2

panic:
  call void @core[dcb47395e1cc1d61]::panicking::panic_bounds_check(i64 noundef %_12, i64 noundef 32, ptr noalias nofree noundef reado

JSON schema ignores `pathlib.WindowsPath` defaults ### Initial Checks - [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2 ### Description `pathlib.WindowsPath` is excluded from `GenerateSchema.match_type` but `pathlib.PosixPath` is not. Is it intentional? https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/ca31682bd9376283f28cc35f66110b7040e7602d/pydantic/_internal/_generate_schema.py#L149-L156 Could not find any info about this but the serialization docs also mention all of them explicitly except `pathlib.WindowsPath` https://pydantic.dev/docs/validation/latest/api/pydantic/standard_library_types#paths This makes the snippet bellow generate different schemas on Unix/Windows since the `Path` constructor actually returns `WindowsPath` or `PosixPath` according to the platform ### Example Code ```Python from pathlib import Path from pydantic import BaseModel class Config(BaseModel): path: Path = Path("config.toml") # default is omitted on Windows, but included on Unix print(Config.model_json_schema()) # PydanticJsonSchemaWarning: Default value config.toml is not JSON serializable; excluding default from JSON schema [non-serializable-default] # PydanticSchemaGenerationError: Unable to generate pydantic-core schema for . # output -> {'properties': {'path': {'format': 'path', 'title': 'Path', 'type': 'string'}}, 'title': 'Config', 'type': 'object'} ``` ### Python, Pydantic & OS Version ```Text pydantic version: 2.13.4 pydantic-core version: 2.46.4 pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false python version: 3.13.12 (main, Mar 10 2026, 18:14:40) [MSC v.1944 64 bit (AMD64)] platform: Windows-11-10.0.26200-SP0 related packages: typing_extensions-4.15.0 commit: unknown ```

Provide a clearer error message when installing a Pydantic version on an unsupported Python version

### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

### Description

I looked in existent issues, but didn't find one related enough to just add a comment.

When trying to install a package that depends on pydantic >=2.10.6,<2.11 with python 3.14, I would expect a dependency resolver failure, because pydantic 2.10.x is not compatible with python 3.14, instead I get a build error.

TITLE: Pydantic 2.13 versioned documentation URL is inaccessible

BODY:
I've encountered a problem with the Pydantic 2.13 documentation. The specific URL for version 2.13, for instance `https://pydantic.dev/docs/validation/2.13/`, does not resolve or display any content. It seems that the documentation for this version is currently only reachable via the `latest` alias, such as `https://pydantic.dev/docs/validation/latest/`.

`model_copy(update=...)` inconsistencies

### Initial Checks

- [x] I confirm that I'm using Pydantic V2

TITLE: Pydantic 2.13.4 Strips `Annotated` Types from Generic Model Fields

BODY:
This report details an issue encountered in Pydantic V2 where `Annotated` type information is lost when used within generic models. The problem manifests after upgrading Pydantic from version 2.12.5 to 2.13.4.

Specifically, when inspecting the `annotation` of a field within a `BaseModel` that itself uses a generic type parameterized with `Annotated`, the `Annotated` wrapper and its metadata are no longer preserved in Pydantic 2.13.4. Instead, only the base type is reflected.

The following example code demonstrates this behavior:

```Python
from typing import TypeVar, Generic, Annotated

from pydantic import BaseModel

T = TypeVar("T")

class ValueWithMetadata(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
    value: T
    metadata: str

class MyModel(BaseModel):
    a: ValueWithMetadata[str]
    b: ValueWithMetadata[Annotated[str, 'my-annotation']]


a_value_annotation = MyModel.model_fields["a"].annotation.model_fields["value"].annotation
b_value_annotation = MyModel.model_fields["b"].annotation.model_fields["value"].annotation
print("a", repr(a_value_annotation))
print("b", repr(b_value_annotation))
```

Running this code produces different outputs depending on the Pydantic version:

**With Pydantic 2.12.5:**
```
a <class 'str'>
b typing.Annotated[str, 'my-annotation']
```

**With Pydantic 2.13.4:**
```
a <class 'str'>
b <class 'str'>
```
As shown, the `Annotated` type for field `b` is completely gone in the newer version.

It has been observed that if the `Annotated` type is wrapped with `Optional` (e.g., `b: ValueWithMetadata[Optional[Annotated[str, 'my-annotation']]]`), the annotation is correctly preserved. It is currently unknown if this issue is related to https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/13085.

**Environment Details:**
```Text
pydantic version: 2.13.4
        pydantic-core version: 2.46.4
          pydantic-core build: profile=release pgo=false
               python version: 3.11.11 (main, Feb  5 2025, 18:58:27) [Clang 19.1.6 ]
                     platform: macOS-14.7.2-arm64-arm-64bit
             related packages: mypy-1.19.1 typing_extensions-4.15.0
                       commit: unknown
```

Performance regression since 1.93 related to MaybeUninit being wrongfully initialized

Since rustc 1.93 (likely #147827), initializing newtype wrapper of a struct that contains an integer type and a MaybeUninit field generates code to initialize the entire struct at -O2, instead of writing only the initialized integer field.

First bad nightly: nightly-2025-11-24 (commit c23ed3ef28b)
Last good nightly: nightly-2025-11-23 (commit 94b49fd99)
Still broken as of: 1.98.0-nightly (2026-06-11, LLVM 22.1.6)

Real-world impact: this caused a 22% performance regression in a specific test in the Firefox performance suite, as reported in https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2046376.
The hot call site creates a struct of three empty SmallVecs (from the smallvec crate with the union feature enabled). With rustc 1.93 the hot path now calls memset for 1224-bytes instead of three 8-byte stores.

### Minimal reproducer

```rust
use std::mem::MaybeUninit;

pub struct Vec { cap: usize, data: MaybeUninit<[u64; 2]> }
pub struct Wrap(Vec);

#[no_mangle]
#[inline(never)]
pub fn make() -> Wrap {
    Wrap(Vec { cap: 0, data: MaybeUninit::uninit() })
}
```
https://godbolt.org/z/KnarEEon8

1.92 generates:
```
make:
        movq    %rdi, %rax
        movq    $0, 16(%rdi)
        retq
```

1.93 generates:
```
make:
        movq    %rdi, %rax
        xorps   %xmm0, %xmm0
        movups  %xmm0, (%rdi)
        movq    $0, 16(%rdi)
        retq
```

With larger arrays in the MaybeUninit, more movups happen, up to the point where it may choose to use memset instead.
IOW, 19.3 is initializing the whole struct instead. Without the wrapping, the MaybeUninit is properly not initialized.

__slots__ is not propagated to generic parameterizations, silently re-adding __weakref__

## Description

`BaseModel.__class_getitem__` synthesizes a new class for each parameterization (`Model[X]`) via `pydantic/_internal/_generics.py::create_generic_submodel`. That function builds the class with a namespace containing only `__module__` (plus what `prepare_class` adds) and never forwards `__slots__` from `origin`. Because the new class body has no `__slots__`, Python's class machinery adds the default `__dict__` and `__weakref__` descriptors. The parent's `__slots__ = ()` is silently discarded for every parameterization.

## Reproducer

```python
import weakref
import pydantic

class Envelope[T](pydantic.BaseModel):
    __slots__ = ()
    content: None | T = None

base = Envelope()
print("base __weakref__:", "__weakref__" in type(base).__dict__)       # False - good
weakref.ref(base)                                                       # TypeError - good

param = Envelope[int]()
print("param __weakref__:", "__weakref__" in type(param).__dict__)     # True  - unexpected
weakref.ref(param)                                                      # succeeds - unexpected

# Even an explicit subclass with __slots__ = () cannot recover, because
# the auto-synthesized intermediate already carries __weakref__:
class Sub(Envelope[int]):
    __slots__ = ()
weakref.ref(Sub())                                                      # succeeds
```

## Expected

`Envelope[int]` (and any user subclass of it) should honor the `__slots__ = ()` declared on the origin and reject `weakref.ref(...)`, matching the un-parameterized `Envelope`.

## Actual

The synthesized `Envelope[int]` class has neither `__slots__` in its own dict nor any inherited declaration that suppresses default storage, so `__weakref__` (and `__dict__`, but `BaseModel` itself forces that one) reappears on every parameterized subclass and on anything inheriting from it.

## Root cause

`pydantic/_internal/_generics.py::create_generic_submodel` constructs the class with:

```python
namespace: dict[str, Any] = {"__module__": origin.__module__}
bases = (origin,)
meta, ns, kwds = prepare_class(model_name, bases)
namespace.update(ns)
created_model = meta(model_name, bases, namespace, ...)
```

`__slots__` is never copied from `origin` into `namespace`, so the synthesized class is declared without it.

## Suggested fix

```python
namespace: dict[str, Any] = {"__module__": origin.__module__}
if "__slots__" in origin.__dict__:
    namespace["__slots__"] = origin.__slots__
```

This propagates the origin's slot declaration to every parameterization. Models that intentionally omit `__slots__` are unaffected. Models that declare `__slots__` (including the common `__slots__ = ()` to drop `__weakref__`) get the behavior they asked for on `Model[X]` as well as on `Model`.

## Workaround

Replace the runtime generic with a `typing.Protocol[T]` and have each concrete envelope inherit `BaseModel` directly. The Protocol parameterization is typing-only, so no intermediate class is synthesized and the concrete classes' `__slots__ = ()` is respected. Costs the parent's shared fields in duplication across concrete subclasses.

## Versions

- pydantic 2.13.4
- Python 3.13

TITLE: Security Risk: `pickle.loads()` Deserialization in Deprecated Pydantic Parsing

BODY:
A potential security vulnerability exists within the deprecated parsing functions in `pydantic/deprecated/parse.py`. Specifically, the `pickle.loads()` method is invoked when `allow_pickle=True` is provided to `load_str_bytes()` or `load_file()`. This allows for deserialization of untrusted data, presenting a significant arbitrary code execution (RCE) risk.

The problematic code segment is located at `pydantic/deprecated/parse.py:55`:
```python
elif proto == Protocol.pickle:
    if not allow_pickle:
        raise RuntimeError('Trying to decode with pickle with allow_pickle=False')
    bb = b if isinstance(b, bytes) else b.encode()
    return pickle.loads(bb)  # ← unsafe on untrusted input
```

The `load_file()` function (line 76) automatically selects `Protocol.pickle` for files with a `.pkl` extension, irrespective of the `allow_pickle` setting. Consequently, any application that uses `load_file()` with `allow_pickle=True` to process files from an attacker-controlled path could be exploited for arbitrary code execution via the `pickle.loads()` call.

This issue is particularly concerning in environments like AI data pipelines (e.g., LangChain, Pydantic AI, FastAPI) where Pydantic is utilized for data modeling and `.pkl` files are commonly used for cached artifacts.

Despite these functions being marked with `@deprecated`, the unsafe pickle deserialization path remains fully accessible. This constitutes a CWE-502 (Deserialization of Untrusted Data) vulnerability, as the deprecation warning does not prevent the execution of the dangerous operation. Callers are vulnerable to RCE if they deserialize untrusted input with `allow_pickle=True`.

meta.original_title: Deprecation cleanup: remove pickle.loads() from pydantic/deprecated/parse.py

rustfmt does not discover modules through `cfg_select!`

In case this issue is a better fit for the rustfmt repo, feel free to move it there. Also, if this is not a good fit for the regression tag, please change it.

Given this `lib.rs` and corresponding module files:

```rust
cfg_match! {
    target_family = "unix" => {
        mod format_me_please_1;
    }
    _ => {
        mod format_me_please_2;
    }
}
```

When formatting this, everything is fine:

```console
$ rustfmt src/lib.rs --verbose
Formatting src/format_me_please_1.rs
Formatting src/format_me_please_2.rs
Formatting src/lib.rs
Spent 0.004 secs in the parsing phase, and 0.002 secs in the formatting phase
```

Support for detecting module files through `cfg_match!` was addded in https://github.com/rust-lang/rustfmt/pull/6522. But `cfg_match!` was renamed to `cfg_select!` in https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/137198, and if you change the code above to say `cfg_select!`, it no longer detects the module files:

```console
$ rustfmt src/lib.rs --verbose
Formatting src/lib.rs
Spent 0.002 secs in the parsing phase, and 0.001 secs in the formatting phase
```

So anyone who has used `cfg_select!` to define modules in other files has possibly unformatted files. This is especially relevant due to the recent stabilization of `cfg_select!` (https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/149783) the migration from `cfg_if!` to `cfg_select!` in the ecosystem (e.g., https://github.com/rust-lang/compiler-builtins/pull/1224).

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/154202 does not appear to fix this issue. Renaming the corresponding occurrences in rustfmt fixes the issue for me, so I have opened https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/158372.

### Description

I've recently tried to bump pydantic version in a project from `2.10.6` to `2.13.4` and it introduced mypy errors related to the usage of an `AliasChoices` as a validation_alias in a  field of a`BaseModel`.

### current mypy config:
```ini
[mypy]
plugins = pydantic.mypy
disallow_any_explicit = true
strict = true

[pydantic-mypy]
init_forbid_extra = true
init_typed = true
warn_required_dynamic_aliases = false
```

### Tests
I've then tested different minor versions to check which one introduced this change, and apparently the next version, `2.11.0a1`, introduced this behavior.

Testing in the example code, we get the following error:
```
test.py:6: error: Explicit "Any" is not allowed  [explicit-any]
```
This seems to indicate that it affects the type checking of the class, and not the typing of the `AliasChoices` itself.

### Questions:
1. Is this is a missing configuration on my part?
1. Is there another way to use validation_alias with multiple options that are properly typed for mypy?

## Related documentation
I've read the documentation about [warn_required_dynamic_aliases](https://pydantic.dev/docs/validation/latest/integrations/dev-tools/mypy/#warn_required_dynamic_aliases) as well which seemed to indicate that the pydantic mypy plugin was properly configured.

### Example Code

```Python
from pydantic import AliasChoices, BaseModel, Field

validation_alias_choices = AliasChoices("choice", "other_choice")


class ModelWithMypyError(BaseModel):
    test_field: str = Field(validation_alias=validation_alias_choices)


class WorkingModel(BaseModel):
    test_field: str = Field(validation_alias="choice")
```

cases.input_text:
TITLE: TypeError when passing custom keyword arguments to `__pydantic_init_subclass__`

BODY:
I'm encountering an issue with Pydantic V2 where custom keyword arguments provided during class definition are not being correctly handled by `__pydantic_init_subclass__`.

The documentation suggests that `__pydantic_init_subclass__` should accept the same keyword arguments as the standard `__init_subclass__`. However, when I define a class like `class MyClass(BaseClass, custom_arg='value')` and override `__pydantic_init_subclass__` to accept `custom_arg`, I receive a `TypeError`.

It appears that the `ModelMetaclass.__new__` method passes all keyword arguments to the parent class's `__new__` first. This triggers the standard `__init_subclass__` in the inheritance chain. If no method in the chain consumes the custom keyword argument (like `event_type` in the example), the default `object.__init_subclass__` raises an error because it doesn't expect extra arguments.

The current workaround involves adding a dummy `__init_subclass__` method to absorb these arguments, but this complicates type checking.

My expectation is that overriding `__pydantic_init_subclass__` should be sufficient to handle custom class-level keyword arguments without needing a separate `__init_subclass__` definition. Alternatively, the documentation could be updated to clarify that `__pydantic_init_subclass__` is not a direct replacement and that custom arguments will require explicit handling via `__init_subclass__`.

**Example:**

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import override

class Event(BaseModel):
    e_type: str

    # The commented-out __init_subclass__ is the workaround
    # @classmethod
    # def __init_subclass__(cls, event_type: str | None = None, **kwargs: object) -> None:
    #     super().__init_subclass__(**kwargs)

    @override
    @classmethod
    def __pydantic_init_subclass__(cls, event_type: str | None = None, **kwargs: object) -> None:
        super().__pydantic_init_subclass__(**kwargs)
        print(f"registered: {event_type}")

class CreatedEvent(Event, event_type='created'): ...
```

This code results in the following error:
`TypeError: CreatedEvent.__init_subclass__() takes no keyword arguments`

**Environment:**
- Pydantic: 2.13.4
- Python: 3.13

meta.original_title: `__pydantic_init_subclass__` cannot receive custom class keyword arguments without also defining `__init_subclass__`

cases.input_text:
Rust 2015 type `dyn + dyn` wrongly rejected by the parser due to "helpful" diagnostic

In Rust 2015, `dyn` is only a *contextual* keyword. It is *only* meant to be active if it appears in a type context and is followed by a token in the following set: {*PathIdentSegment*, *LIFETIME_OR_LABEL*, `for`, `(`, `?`} as dictated by [`can_begin_dyn_bound_in_edition_2015`](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/83adf7e080d0fe4f0970c00eac2976a767dbd042/compiler/rustc_parse/src/parser/ty.rs#L88-L103) (cc https://github.com/rust-lang/reference/pull/2257) (side note: [RFC 2113](https://rust-lang.github.io/rfcs/2113-dyn-trait-syntax.html) doesn't actually define the rules).

Despite that fact, since 1.54 rustc rejects the following Rust 2015 program:

```rs
#[cfg(false)]
type X = dyn + dyn;
```

```
error: invalid `dyn` keyword
 --> src/lib.rs:2:16
  |
2 | type X = dyn + dyn;
  |                ^^^
  |
  = help: `dyn` is only needed at the start of a trait `+`-separated list
help: remove this keyword
  |
2 - type X = dyn + dyn;
2 + type X = dyn + ;
  |

error: expected identifier, found `;`
 --> src/lib.rs:2:19
  |
2 | type X = dyn + dyn;
  |                   ^ expected identifier
```

More concretely, rustc erroneously rejects this code since PR #84896 which only meant to improve diagnostics, not alter the stable grammar of Rust.

Note that `dyn + dyn` *can* be semantically well-formed, too (assuming this parser bug has been fixed). Consider the following contrived Rust 2015 code:

```rs
use std::marker::Sync as dyn;
type Y = dyn + dyn;
```
